curriculumData: 1. Basic Anatomy and Physiology of Bone
2. Basic Terminology of Fracture and Healing

qnaData.stem:
A 24 year old male comes to emergency department with a history of road traffic accident. He complains of pain and bleeding from his right leg. On examination there is laceration 2cm X 3cm over the shin of right tibia with fracture of the right leg. His distal pulses are palpable.
• a) What is your diagnosis?
• b) Name the classification system used for this type of fracture. Write down the different types of fracture as per this system of classification.
• c) What are the immediate complications of this fracture?
• d) What are the late complications of this fracture?
• e) Write the investigations to be done for this case.
• f) What is the implant of choice for the fracture? [1+2+2+1+1+1=8]

Define Fracture neck of femur.
• How do you classify them?
• Write down its clinical features.
• What are its differential diagnosis?
• How will you confirm your diagnosis?
• Give the treatment plan for fresh fracture neck of femur. [1+1+1+1+1+2=7]
SAQ: Crush Syndrome [5]
SAQ: Gunstock deformity [5]
SAQ: Monteggia fracture [5]
SAQ: Meniscal injuries of Knee [5]

Define Osteomyelitis.
• What are its different types?
• What are the clinical features of acute osteomyelitis?
• What are the common organisms causing osteomyelitis in various age groups?
• Write the differential diagnosis for acute osteomyelitis?
• Give the treatment plan for acute osteomyelitis.
• What are the complications of acute osteomyelitis? [1+1+1+1+1+1+1+1=8]

A 38 year male come to OPD with a history of gradual onset backache since 3 months. He gives a history of low grade fever with loss of body weight. He has a past history of pulmonary tuberculosis in childhood for which he had taken 6 months ATT. On examination, he has a kyphus in dorsal spine and his knee jerks are exaggerated with ill sustained clonus. His motor power is normal and he has no bowel or bladder involvement.
• A) What is your probable diagnosis?
• B) What are the differential diagnosis?
• C) What are the causes of paraplegia in this disease?
• D) Write the staging of the disease?
• E) What are the investigation to be done for the patient?
• F) Give the treatment plan for the patient.
• G) What are the complications of the disease? [1+1+1+1+1+1+1=7]

qnaData.stem:
A 6-yr child comes to OPD with a history of low grade fever and loss of appetite since 5 months. He has enlarged inguinal lymph nodes and the right lower limb is in attitude of flexion, abduction and external rotation. He has tenderness around the hip joint.
• What is your diagnosis?
• Classify the stages of this disease.
• What will be its management? [1+2+4=7]

A 24-year-old man met an accident. He fell from a motorbike on the road and sustained a wound over his left leg. He could not move his injured limb. After he was taken to the hospital, he was diagnosed with a fracture of the left tibia and fibula. The clinician found that his posterior tibial artery and dorsalis pedis were not palpable and the foot was cold.
• a) What is his diagnosis?
• b) How do you grade the fracture?
• c) How do you manage this patient?
• d) What are the possible complications? [2+2+2+2=8]

An elderly female fell in her kitchen and experienced sudden onset pain in her right groin region. She could not stand up and was taken to the hospital on a stretcher by her son. The duty doctor observed her right limb to be 3 cm shorter and externally rotated. She had swelling in her anterior hip region. The distal neurovascular examination was normal.
• a) What is your diagnosis?
• b) How do you classify these fractures?
• c) How do you manage the condition?
• d) What are its complications? [1+2+2+2=7]

• how is Pott's paraplegia classified clinically,

qnaData.stem: Concerning periosteum, which of the statements is false?
options: Periosteum is thicker in children than in adults
Periosteum is continuous with the joint capsule
Periosteum provides the predominant blood Supplementaryy to bone in adults
Periosteum is made of two layers

options: Lamellar cortical bone is the least dense bone type.
Lamellar cancellous bone is the strongest type of bone.
Lamellar cancellous bone has a honeycomb appearance and contains many cells.
Lamellar bone is the principal bone type in the neonate.

A 50-year-old man presents with a stiff painful knee. He has had no treatment to date. He works as a chef and has a BMI of 40. He is otherwise fit and well. Examination reveals a range of movement 0–120° with mild crepitus throughout. Alignment of the knee is normal. X-rays show early osteoarthritis of medial and lateral compartments. The first-line treatment should include:
options: Physiotherapy, activity modification and lifestyle changes

A 26-year-old rugby player experiences recurrent anterior dislocations of his shoulder following a rugby injury 2 years ago. His shoulder now dislocates whenever he externally rotates the shoulder beyond 30°. No other joints are unstable and there is no family history of joint instability. A CT shows that the anterior one-third of the glenoid has been fractured off. There is no defect in the humeral head. The recurrent dislocation is most likely due to:
Tennis elbow affects :
options: The common flexor origin of the elbow
The common extensor origin of the elbow

qnaData.stem: A patient develops compartment syndrome (swelling, pain and numbness) following manipulation and plaster for fracture of both bones of leg. What is the best treatment?
options: Infusion of low molecular weight dextran
Elevate the leg after splitting the plaster
Do operative decompression of fascial compartment

qnaData.stem: A patient had injury to the upper limb 3 yrs earlier, now he presents with paresthesia over the medial border of the hand and anaesthesia over medial two finger. The injury is likely to have been:

options: Fracture of the neck of the femur
Fracture of the shaft of the femur
Rheumatoid arthritis of the hip joint

Examination revealed low grade fever with marked tenderness over the spine, ribs, sternum, skull and pelvic bones.
